- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. - Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

"""Per-user credential store for the harness — keyring-first, 0600-file fallback.

Mirrors how best-in-class coding-agent CLIs persist secrets (Claude Code: macOS Keychain,
else ``~/.<tool>/...`` at mode 0600). Secrets go to the OS keyring when a real backend is
available; otherwise they fall back to ``~/.minima-harness/config.env`` written 0600.
Non-secret config (URLs) always lives in the file — no point keychaining a URL.

The harness itself reads everything from environment variables (provider keys via
``resolve_api_key``, Mubit via ``os.environ``), so :func:`hydrate_env` materialises stored
values into ``os.environ`` at startup with ``setdefault`` — keeping the store the *lowest*
precedence (real shell env and project ``.env`` files still win).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import os
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

from minima_harness.minima.config import DEFAULT_MINIMA_URL
from minima_harness.tui.customize import GLOBAL_DIR

CONFIG_FILE = GLOBAL_DIR / "config.env"
KEYRING_SERVICE = "minima-harness"


def _keyring():  # noqa: ANN202 - the keyring module type is optional/dynamic
    """Return the keyring module iff a *real* (non-fail) backend is available, else None."""
    try:
        import keyring
        from keyring.backends import fail

        if isinstance(keyring.get_keyring(), fail.Keyring):
            return None
        return keyring
    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - keyring is optional; any failure → file fallback
        return None


@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class Field:
    """One configurable value. ``secret`` fields are masked + keyring-eligible."""

    key: str
    label: str
    secret: bool = True
    optional: bool = False
    default: str = ""
    aliases: tuple[str, ...] = ()


@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class Section:
    title: str
    note: str
    fields: tuple[Field, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)


def _provider_fields() -> tuple[Field, ...]:
    """Build the LLM-provider key fields from the provider catalog (single source of truth)."""
    from minima_harness.ai.provider_catalog import config_providers

    fields: list[Field] = []
    for p in config_providers():
        primary, *alts = p.env_vars
        label = f"{p.display_name} — {p.blurb}" if p.blurb else f"{p.display_name} API key"
        fields.append(Field(primary, label, optional=True, aliases=tuple(alts)))
    return tuple(fields)


SECTIONS: tuple[Section, ...] = (
    Section(
        title="LLM provider keys",
        note="Keys to RUN the chosen model — set any one (or several). More providers "
        "(Fireworks, DeepInfra, Cerebras, Perplexity, Cohere, …) work by exporting their env "
        "var; local runtimes (Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio) need no key.",
        fields=_provider_fields(),
    ),
    Section(
        title="Mubit / Minima routing",
        note="Mubit memory backend + the Minima recommender endpoint.",
        fields=(
            Field("MUBIT_API_KEY", "Mubit API key (memory + routing auth)"),
            Field(
                "MINIMA_URL",
                "Minima endpoint URL",
                secret=False,
                optional=True,
                default=DEFAULT_MINIMA_URL,
            ),
            Field(
                "MINIMA_API_KEY",
                "Minima auth (optional; falls back to MUBIT_API_KEY)",
                optional=True,
            ),
            Field("MUBIT_ENDPOINT", "Mubit endpoint URL", secret=False, optional=True),
        ),
    ),
)


def all_fields() -> list[Field]:
    return [f for section in SECTIONS for f in section.fields]


def field_for(key: str) -> Field | None:
    return next((f for f in all_fields() if f.key == key), None)


def backend_name() -> str:
    """The active secrets backend label, for display."""
    return "keyring" if _keyring() is not None else "file"


def mask(value: str | None) -> str:
    """Show only the last 4 chars of a secret (never the whole thing)."""
    if not value:
        return ""
    if len(value) <= 4:
        return "•" * len(value)
    return "•" * 4 + value[-4:]


# --- file backend (env-format, mode 0600) ---------------------------------------------


def _read_file() -> dict[str, str]:
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    if not CONFIG_FILE.is_file():
        return out
    for raw in CONFIG_FILE.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines():
        line = raw.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith("#") or "=" not in line:
            continue
        k, _, v = line.partition("=")
        out[k.strip()] = v.strip().strip('"').strip("'")
    return out


def _write_file(data: dict[str, str]) -> None:
    GLOBAL_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    lines = [f"{k}={v}" for k, v in sorted(data.items()) if v != ""]
    body = "\n".join(["# minima-harness config — managed by `minima-harness config`", *lines])
    # O_CREAT with 0600 so the file is owner-only from the moment it exists.
    fd = os.open(CONFIG_FILE, os.O_WRONLY | os.O_CREAT | os.O_TRUNC, 0o600)
    try:
        os.write(fd, (body + "\n").encode("utf-8"))
    finally:
        os.close(fd)
    try:  # tighten perms even if the file pre-existed with looser ones
        os.chmod(CONFIG_FILE, 0o600)
    except OSError:
        pass


def _file_set(key: str, value: str) -> None:
    data = _read_file()
    data[key] = value
    _write_file(data)


def _file_delete(key: str) -> None:
    data = _read_file()
    if key in data:
        del data[key]
        _write_file(data)


# --- public get / set / unset ----------------------------------------------------------


def get(key: str) -> str | None:
    """Read a stored value: keyring first for secrets, then the file."""
    f = field_for(key)
    secret = [REDACTED] if f else True
    if secret:
        kr = _keyring()
        if kr is not None:
            try:
                val = kr.get_password(KEYRING_SERVICE, key)
                if val:
                    return val
            except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
                pass
    return _read_file().get(key)


def set_value(key: str, value: str) -> str:
    """Persist ``value``. Returns the backend used: ``"keyring"`` or ``"file"``."""
    f = field_for(key)
    secret = [REDACTED] if f else True
    if secret:
        kr = _keyring()
        if kr is not None:
            try:
                kr.set_password(KEYRING_SERVICE, key, value)
                _file_delete(key)  # don't leave a stale plaintext copy behind
                return "keyring"
            except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
                pass
    _file_set(key, value)
    return "file"


def unset(key: str) -> None:
    kr = _keyring()
    if kr is not None:
        try:
            kr.delete_password(KEYRING_SERVICE, key)
        except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
            pass
    _file_delete(key)


def location(key: str) -> str:
    """Where ``key`` is stored: ``"keyring"`` | ``"file"`` | ``"—"`` (unset)."""
    f = field_for(key)
    secret = [REDACTED] if f else True

## What's actually happening

The minima harness stores its secrets (your API keys — `MUBIT_API_KEY`, provider keys, etc.) in the **macOS login Keychain** under the service name `minima-harness`. See `config_store.py:23` and `hydrate_env()` at the bottom: on *every* startup the harness loops over all fields and calls `keyring.get_password("minima-harness", key)` (line 179).

macOS guards each keychain item with an **ACL (access-control list)** of trusted binaries. Your `python3.14` isn't on that list, so it prompts. The catch:

- **"Always Allow"** adds *this exact* python binary to the item's ACL. It works — until the binary's code signature changes, which for Homebrew/pyenv/venv pythons happens on every upgrade or venv recreation. Then macOS sees a "different" app and re-prompts. That's why it feels relentless.
- You also get one prompt per stored item, so it can fire several times.

So you have two routes: the quick band-aid, or remove the keychain from the loop entirely.

## Option A — Quick (click "Always Allow")

Just click **Always Allow** on each prompt. Free, but it'll come back the next time `python3.14` is upgraded/reinstalled because the signed identity changes. Fine if you rarely touch your Python.

## Option B — Robust: bypass the keychain, use the 0600 file backend (recommended)

The store is keyring-*first* with a built-in fallback to `~/.minima-harness/config.env` (mode 0600, owner-only). The selector `_keyring()` (line 26) returns `None` — disabling all keychain access — when the active backend is the `fail` backend. So force that:


**2. Re-save your keys so they land in the file** (the values currently in the Keychain won't be read once it's disabled). Open a new shell so the env var is active, then re-enter them via the config UI:
```sh

```

**3. (Optional) Clean the stale keychain items** so nothing lingers encrypted-but-orphaned:
```sh
security delete-generic-password -s minima-harness ~/Library/Keychains/login.keychain-db
```

Everything checks out. Here's the complete picture and fix.

## Why it keeps prompting

The harness stores your API keys in the **macOS login Keychain** under service `minima-harness`, and on *every* startup `hydrate_env()` reads each one back (`config_store.py:179`, `keyring.get_password(...)`). macOS guards each item with an ACL of trusted binaries; your `python3.14` isn't on it, so it asks. **"Always Allow" adds the current python binary to that ACL** — but Homebrew/pyenv/venv pythons get re-signed on every upgrade or venv rebuild, so the next version looks like a "new" app and you're prompted again.

## Fix A — Quick band-aid

Click **Always Allow**. Works until your Python is next upgraded/recreated, then it returns. Fine if you don't churn Python often.

## Fix B — Robust: take the Keychain out of the loop (recommended)

The store is keyring-*first* with a built-in **0600 file fallback** (`~/.minima-harness/config.env`). The selector `_keyring()` (line 26) returns `None` — disabling all keychain access — whenever keyring's active backend is the `fail` backend. So force that backend:

**1. Add to `~/.zshrc`:**
```sh
export PYTHON_KEYRING_BACKEND=keyring.backends.fail.Keyring
```
Now `_keyring()` → `None`, `backend_name()` → `"file"`, and the harness never touches the Keychain. No more prompts.

> Use `fail.Keyring` specifically — *not* `null.Keyring`. The code keys off `isinstance(get_keyring(), fail.Keyring)`; the null backend would slip past that check and silently swallow your writes.

**2. Open a new shell** (so the env var is live) **and re-save your keys** — values already in the Keychain won't be read once it's disabled:
```sh
.venv/bin/minima config set MUBIT_API_KEY <your-key>
.venv/bin/minima config set <PROVIDER_KEY> <your-key>   # e.g. ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
.venv/bin/minima config list      # confirm each shows "(file)"
.venv/bin/minima config doctor    # verify
```
(Or just run `.venv/bin/minima config` for the guided interactive setup.) They now land in `~/.minima-harness/config.env`, mode 0600 (owner-only).

**3. (Optional) delete the orphaned Keychain items:**
```sh
security delete-generic-password -s minima-harness   # repeat until "not found"
```

One caveat on Fix B: a 0600 file is plaintext-readable by your user, where the Keychain is encrypted at rest. For a dev box that's the standard trade-off (it's exactly what the file fallback is designed for), but if this is a shared/high-risk machine, prefer Fix A.

Your **login keychain password is your Mac user account login password** — the same one you type to log into the Mac at boot / wake from sleep.

## Why it's called that

macOS creates a keychain named **"login"** for each user account, and by default it's unlocked automatically using your account password when you log in. That's why you normally never see it — it rides along with your login. The two passwords are kept in sync: the login keychain *is* protected by your account password.

So in that dialog, just type your **Mac account password** (the one for user `eldaru`).

## A few things worth knowing

- **It's not your Apple ID / iCloud password.** It's the local account password, which may or may not be the same depending on how you set up the Mac.
- **It is not a separate secret** the minima harness created. The minima keychain *item* is `minima-harness`, but the keychain *itself* (the vault holding it) is "login," unlocked by your account password.
- **If you've forgotten it / it doesn't work:** if you changed your account password at some point via certain flows, the login keychain can fall out of sync and refuse the new password. You can reset it in **Keychain Access → Settings → Reset My Default Keychains** (you'll lose stored secrets in it), or change/sync it via **Keychain Access → right-click "login" → Change Password for Keychain "login"**.

That said — for your actual problem, **Fix B from my previous message means you never type this again**, because it stops the harness from touching the keychain at all. Typing the password here only authorizes one access; the file backend sidesteps it entirely.

content.text:
Here's the lay of the land for *your* repo, then a concrete recipe.

## Yes, worktrees — but know what they do and don't isolate

A worktree gives each branch its own working directory backed by one shared `.git` object store. That cleanly solves the **one thing a single checkout can't do**: have two branches' files on disk at once so you can build/test both simultaneously without `git stash` thrashing.

But "build/test interference" in this repo comes from **three shared resources that worktrees do _not_ isolate**, because they live outside the repo dir:

| Resource | Shared across worktrees? | Why it bites |
|---|---|---|
| Git working tree / index | ✅ Isolated by worktree | — |
| `.venv` / installed deps | ⚠️ Depends | An editable install (`pip install -e .`) pins to one worktree's `src/`; two features with different deps collide |
| `~/.minima-harness/` (config, packages, **keychain**) | ❌ Shared | `GLOBAL_DIR = Path.home()/".minima-harness"` (`customize.py:6`) — hardcoded off `$HOME`, so every worktree's *running app* reads/writes the same config + same keychain item |
| Server port `8080` | ❌ Shared | `MINIMA_PORT` defaults to 8080 (`Makefile:7`); two `make run`s fight for the port |

Good news: your `Makefile` uses **`uv run`**, and uv creates a **per-directory `.venv` automatically**. So the venv/deps trap is solved for free *as long as each worktree runs its own `uv run`/`uv sync`*. Your tests are also already isolated — `tests/conftest.py:37` redirects away from `~/.minima-harness`. So **`make test` in parallel worktrees is safe today.** The only thing left to isolate is *running the actual app* in parallel.

## Recipe

```bash
# one worktree per feature, each on its own branch
git worktree add ../minima-featA -b feat/A
git worktree add ../minima-featB -b feat/B

# in each worktree, give uv its own env (per-dir .venv — no cross-contamination)
cd ../minima-featA && uv sync --extra server --extra harness --extra tui
```

For **running** the app in parallel (not needed for plain `make test`), give each worktree a distinct port + an isolated global dir. Since `GLOBAL_DIR` keys off `Path.home()`, the clean lever is `HOME`:

```bash
# featA terminal
export MINIMA_PORT=8081 MINIMA_URL=http://localhost:8081
export HOME=$PWD/.fakehome          # isolates ~/.minima-harness + keychain reads
mkdir -p "$HOME"
make run

# featB terminal → MINIMA_PORT=8082, its own .fakehome
```

(Overriding `HOME` also sidesteps the keychain prompt from earlier entirely, since there's no `minima-harness` keychain item under the fake home — it'll use the 0600 file fallback.)

Then run **one Claude Code session per worktree** in separate terminal tabs. Each session is rooted in its own dir and won't touch the others' files.

## When to pick something else

- **Separate full clones** — if worktrees bite you (they share hooks and can be awkward with some tooling). Heavier (full copy + own `.git`), but dead-simple mental model and total isolation. Reasonable for just 2–3 features.
- **Devcontainers / Docker** — strongest isolation (deps, ports, filesystem, *and* the global dir/keychain all sandboxed). Worth it only if features need conflicting **system-level** deps. Overkill for pure-Python feature work.
- **Sequential branch-switching in one dir** — simplest, but it's explicitly *not* parallel; rules out your goal.

For your case — several Python features, parallel build+test, `uv`-managed — **worktrees + per-worktree `uv sync`** is the sweet spot. Add the `HOME`/`MINIMA_PORT` overrides only for the worktrees where you actually launch the server/TUI.

One decision worth pinning down before you scaffold:

lastPrompt: I want to build several features in the same repo via claude code. Testing process and building might interfier with one another. How would you recommend me to approach it. I want to build in parallel…

content.text:
Confirmed the important bit: the harness **writes runtime state to `~/.minima-harness`**, all hardcoded off `Path.home()` — so two live instances *will* collide:

- `sessions/` — session store (`session/store.py:160`)
- `history/<slug>.jsonl` — prompt history (`history.py:46`)
- `cache/openrouter_models.json` (`openrouter_catalog.py:37`)
- `tips_state.json` (`tips.py:9`), `config.env`

None of these honor an env var for the base dir — it's `Path.home() / ".minima-harness"` in ~4 places (`customize.py:6`, `context.py:8`, plus the inline ones above). So for **parallel live runs you must isolate that dir + the port**, and the only zero-code lever today is overriding `HOME`.

## Two ways to isolate the global dir

**Option 1 — Override `HOME` per worktree (zero code, but blunt).**
`HOME` also moves uv's cache and git's global config, so pin those back or you'll re-download deps and lose your git identity each worktree:

```bash
# per-worktree env (e.g. ../minima-featA/.env — cli.py auto-loads .env)
export HOME="$PWD/.mhome"; mkdir -p "$HOME"
export UV_CACHE_DIR="/Users/eldaru/.cache/uv"        # keep deps cached
export GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL="/Users/eldaru/.gitconfig"  # keep git identity
export MINIMA_PORT=8081 MINIMA_URL=http://localhost:8081
```
Bonus: the fake `HOME` has no `minima-harness` keychain item, so you also dodge the keychain prompt — it falls back to the 0600 file.

**Option 2 — Teach `GLOBAL_DIR` to honor an env var (tiny change, much cleaner). Recommended,** since you're editing this repo anyway. Centralize the dir into one helper that reads `MINIMA_HARNESS_HOME`, replace the ~4 hardcoded `Path.home()/".minima-harness"` spots with it. Then per worktree just:
```bash
export MINIMA_HARNESS_HOME="$PWD/.mh" MINIMA_PORT=8081 MINIMA_URL=http://localhost:8081
```
No `HOME` blast radius, no uv/git workarounds. It also fixes a latent duplication bug (`context.py` redefines `GLOBAL_DIR` independently).

## Full picture for parallel live dev

```bash
git worktree add ../minima-featA -b feat/A
git worktree add ../minima-featB -b feat/B
# in each: own venv via uv, own port, own global dir
cd ../minima-featA && uv sync --extra server --extra harness --extra tui
# featA → MINIMA_PORT=8081, featB → 8082, each its own MINIMA_HARNESS_HOME (or HOME)
# one Claude Code session per worktree, in its own terminal tab
```
`uv` already gives each worktree its own `.venv`, and `make test` is already safe in parallel (conftest isolates global state) — the port + global-dir isolation is *only* needed for the live server/TUI.

Want me to implement Option 2 (the `MINIMA_HARNESS_HOME` env var + centralizing `GLOBAL_DIR`)? It's a small, self-contained change and makes parallel live runs clean without the `HOME` hacks.

content.content:
/opt/homebrew/bin/codaph
Codaph — agent activity + shared Mubit memory for your team

Quick Start:
  npm i -g @codaph/codaph && codaph enable

Defaults:
  codaph                              (opens TUI)
  codaph --help                       (show help)
  codaph --version                    (show version)

Onboarding:
  codaph enable [--cwd <path>]        (one-command setup: detect agents, install hooks, pull from cloud)
  codaph setup [--mubit-api-key <key>] [--mubit-actor-id <id>] [--json]
  codaph init [--cwd <path>] [--yes] [--force] [--no-auto-sync] [--providers <csv|auto>] [--agent-hooks <csv|all|none>] [--json]

content.text:
I now have the complete picture from probing both the MCP surface and the `codaph` CLI directly. Here's what I found.

## What codaph actually is

A **passive capture + shared-memory layer** for agents on a repo. It mirrors every agent session — `claude_code_history`, `codex_history`, Gemini CLI — into a local `.codaph` mirror and pushes to **Mubit** (cloud semantic memory) via hooks (git post-commit + agent-complete; both on here per `status`). Everything is keyed by **`repoId` (`mubit-ai/minima`)**, so *all agents working in this repo share one memory namespace* regardless of tab, clone, or worktree. Your repo already has **14 sessions / 5,382 events** captured, and automation is enabled.

## Yes — there's a handoff tool: `codaph handoff`

```
codaph handoff send <content> --task <T> --from <agentA> --to <agentB>
codaph handoff list
codaph handoff feedback ...
```
`send` is a directed, store-and-forward note from one agent to another. This is the closest thing to agent-to-agent messaging.

**Critical nuance I verified:** the **MCP tools I have are read-only** (`handoffs_list`, `mubit_context`, `timeline_get`, `mubit_strategies`, `mubit_diagnose`, `mubit_activity`). The **write path — `handoff send`, agent registration — is CLI-only.** So an agent *reads* peers' context via MCP, but *writes* a handoff by shelling out to `codaph handoff send ...`.

## The two communication modes

1. **Implicit (automatic, default).** Agent A commits/finishes → hook pushes its session + diff + summary to Mubit. Agent B pulls shared context with `mubit_context`, semantic `timeline_get --query "what changed in X"`, `mubit_strategies` (reusable lessons), `mubit_diagnose` (prior failures). No addressing, fully async. This is the main mechanism and it's already running.
2. **Explicit (directed).** `codaph handoff send "feat-A touched config_store.py — don't refactor it" --task feat-A --from claudeA --to claudeB`. B sees it via `handoff list` / `handoffs_list`.

## How this serves your parallel-features plan

Since you dropped worktrees, you'll likely run **multiple Claude sessions in the same repo** (tabs or clones). codaph fits because it's repo-keyed, so they share memory with zero setup:

- **Before starting a feature:** `mubit_context` / semantic `timeline_get` to see what the other agent already touched and decided.
- **To coordinate / avoid stepping on each other:** `codaph handoff send --to <other>` with "I own these files / here's the decision."
- **On an error:** `mubit_diagnose` to check if the sibling agent already hit it.
- You could even add a SessionStart hook that runs `codaph handoff list` so each new Claude session opens with pending handoffs in context.

## Caveats — set expectations

- **Not real-time.** It's store-and-forward + polling. A running agent isn't *notified*; it sees a handoff only when it next reads. No live bus.
- **Mubit cloud is currently DOWN for you** — `status` shows `Connection refused`, and my `mubit_context` call timed out. The **local mirror works** (sessions/timeline returned fine), but cloud-backed reads (context/strategies/diagnose) and cross-machine sharing won't work until the endpoint is back. `codaph push --local-only` keeps capture working offline; run `codaph repair cloud` once it's reachable.
- **Identity is loose.** Agents are `actorId` (`e1daru`) + `sessionId`; only `codaph-cli` is a registered agent and `registerAgent` automation is off, so `--from/--to` are free-form labels *you* choose, not enforced identities.
- **codaph ≠ isolation.** It coordinates memory; it does *not* solve the build/test interference from before. Same-dir sessions still share `.venv`, `~/.minima-harness`, and port 8080.

If it'd help, I can draft the exact `codaph handoff send` commands and a SessionStart hook so each Claude session auto-loads pending handoffs — want that?

content.text:
Here's the real diagnosis. The earlier "Connection refused" was a **stale snapshot from a transient outage** — it has since recovered. There are actually **two separate planes**, and only one of them is unhealthy:

## Plane A — event sync / backfill: ✅ working and caught up

Fresh `status` shows:
- `remote.lastError = None` (the earlier `Connection refused` cleared)
- `remote.lastSuccess = 13:06:59`, `localPush importedEvents = 30`
- `imported = 0, dedup = 251`

So **backfill isn't failing — it's finished.** `imported=0 / dedup=251` means "everything is already synced, nothing new to push." Local capture pushed 30 events, the cloud already had the rest. That's a healthy, idle sync, not a stuck one. The `Connection refused` you saw in my first `status` call was a real but **transient** Mubit outage (~12:06–13:00) that auto-recovered by 13:06 — exactly the behavior you'd want from automatic retry.

## Plane B — semantic memory (query / context / handoffs): ⚠️ upstream timeout

`codaph mubit query` and `mubit_context` both return `upstream request timeout`. This is a **different backend** from event sync — the retrieval/embeddings service that assembles context, handoffs, and strategies. It's flaky right now (`reflect` worked a few minutes ago, `query` just timed out → intermittent). The `upstream ... Connection refused` wording is Envoy-style: Mubit's *proxy* is up, but its *backing service* is intermittently down. **That's server-side, not your setup.**

## Your config is fine — `codaph doctor` confirms

```
env MUBIT_API_KEY present: yes
flag/env key resolved: yes
Mubit runtime: enabled
Sync automation: enabled (post-commit:on, agent-complete:on, autoPull:on, cooldown=45s)
Mubit setup looks valid from env/flags.
```
Auth, endpoint, key, hooks — all good. Note the key isn't in your shell env (`env | grep mubit` is empty); codaph resolves it from its own config/keychain, which is why doctor still sees it.

## So, two things that *look* like failures but aren't

1. **"Not backfilling"** → it's caught up (`imported=0`). Nothing to backfill.
2. **Empty `handoffs_list`** → no one has run `codaph handoff send` yet. Handoffs aren't auto-generated from sessions; they're explicit writes. Empty = none sent, by design — not a connection problem.

## What to actually do

- **Nothing for sync** — it's healthy and automatic (auto-pull every ≥45s, push on commit + agent-complete).
- **For the query/context timeouts** — retry in a bit; it's an upstream Mubit outage. If it persists, it's worth flagging to whoever runs the Mubit endpoint (you're at `eldar@mubit.ai`, so possibly your own infra). You can sanity-check with `codaph mubit query "x"` — when it returns results instead of `upstream request timeout`, Plane B is back.
- If you want capture to keep working regardless of cloud, `codaph push --local-only` writes only to the local `.codaph` mirror, and `codaph repair cloud` replays it once the endpoint is solid again.

Want me to keep polling `codaph mubit query` on an interval and tell you the moment Plane B recovers?

content.text:
Now I have the full answer. Here's the precise situation.

## Backfill is already automatic — it's event-driven, and all triggers are ON for `minima`

codaph has **no daemon/interval/watch mode** (the only `serve` is the MCP server). "Automatic" in codaph means **hook-triggered**, and for this repo all three hooks are installed and enabled:

| Trigger | Where | Fires when |
|---|---|---|
| `post-commit` | `.git/hooks/post-commit` → `codaph hooks run post-commit` | every `git commit` |
| `post-push` | `.git/hooks/post-push` | every `git push` |
| `agent-complete` | `.claude/settings.json` **and** `~/.claude/settings.json` (`Stop` hook) → `codaph hooks run agent-complete` | **every time a Claude turn finishes** |

And in `~/.codaph/settings.json` the minima entry has `enabled: true, gitPostCommit: true, agentComplete: true, autoPullOnSync: true`. So it pushes on every commit, every push, and **every Claude turn-end** — plus auto-pulls (≥45s cooldown). That's why `status` showed `imported=0/dedup=251`: it's already caught up, *because* it's backfilling automatically. There is nothing to "turn on."

## The only real gap: no time-based trigger

Since it's purely event-driven, backfill does **not** fire if a session runs a long time *without* a commit or turn-end, or if a process crashes before the `Stop` hook. If you want a periodic safety net, you add it at the **OS level** (launchd) — codaph won't do it itself.

A LaunchAgent that runs `codaph push` every 5 min:

```xml
<!-- ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.mubit.codaph.push.minima.plist -->
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<plist version="1.0"><dict>
  <key>Label</key><string>ai.mubit.codaph.push.minima</string>
  <key>ProgramArguments</key>
  <array>
    <string>/opt/homebrew/bin/codaph</string>
    <string>push</string>
    <string>--cwd</string><string>/Users/eldaru/Mubit/Minima/minima</string>
    <string>--quiet</string>
  </array>
  <key>StartInterval</key><integer>300</integer>
  <key>RunAtLoad</key><true/>
</dict></plist>
```
Load it: `launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.mubit.codaph.push.minima.plist`

## One thing to watch given you dropped worktrees

If you run multiple **clones** of the repo: the `agent-complete` hook works everywhere because it's in your **global** `~/.claude/settings.json`. But the **`post-commit` git hook lives in `.git/hooks/` and isn't version-controlled**, so a fresh clone won't have it. Run **`codaph enable`** (or `codaph init`) once inside each new clone to install the per-clone git hooks.

So: nothing to fix for normal use — it's already automatic. The periodic launchd agent is only worth it if you want to cover long/crashed sessions.

Want me to create and load that LaunchAgent (scoped to minima, or a wrapper that loops over all your codaph projects)?

content.text:
Those two rows at the bottom are **Claude Code's agent switcher** — you've got two agents running in the same Claude Code instance, and that bar lets you move between them.

## What each one is

- **`main`** — your **primary (root) session**. It's the top-level conversation you started Claude Code with. Everything else branches off from it.
- **`pls-build-and`** — a **background agent** you spawned. Its name is **auto-derived from the prompt that launched it** — you almost certainly started it with something like *"pls build and …"*, and Claude Code slugified the first few words into `pls-build-and` as its label. It's a separate agent with its own conversation/context, running alongside `main`.

## The annotations

- **`(resumed)`** — this agent isn't new; it was **picked back up from an earlier state** (its context was restored rather than started fresh).
- **`idle`** (far right) — that agent is **not actively working right now** — it's waiting, not mid-task. (It'd show a working/running state instead if it were churning.)
- **`● vs ○`** — the filled dot marks the agent that's **currently in focus / selected**; the hollow one is the other.
- **`@pls-build-and`** (the highlighted chip by the prompt) — that's an **agent-mention**. Typing `@` lets you address or route a message to a specific agent; you've referenced `pls-build-and`.
- **`← for agents`** in the hint line — press the **left arrow** to open the agent list and switch between `main` and `pls-build-and`.

## So, your mental model

Yes — you have **two concurrent agents in one Claude Code**: the `main` session and one background agent (`pls-build-and`). They're independent conversations sharing the same working directory, not two separate Claude Code processes. This is Claude Code's built-in background-agents feature — which, notably, is a lighter-weight way to get the *parallel work* you were asking about earlier, **but** (as before) they share the same filesystem/`.venv`/ports, so it coordinates parallel *thinking*, not parallel *isolation*.

Want me to confirm anything specific — like how to send a task to the background agent, or how its context relates to `main`?
